explicit_text: I was born in Berlin in the early 1930s. It was a turbulent time as the political climate was rapidly changing. My family decided to leave Berlin in 1938 to escape the dangers posed by Adolf Hitler's regime. That move shaped much of my early life and future.

explicit_text: During World War II, I was deeply involved in intelligence work. I trained at Camp Ritchie and was deployed to several key locations across Europe, including the United Kingdom, Paris, Belgium, Luxembourg, Germany, and Scotland. The war shaped my early adult years and gave me unique experiences that influenced my later career.

explicit_text: After the war, I pursued higher education at the University of Southern California. It was there that I laid the groundwork for my legal studies. USC provided me with a strong academic foundation before I went on to Harvard Law School.

explicit_text: I joined the law firm McCutchen, Doyle, Brown & Enersen in 1952 and worked there until 1976. During my time as a trial lawyer, I handled antitrust litigation and worked on cases involving major companies like Shell Oil, Greyhound Corporation, and Lucky Lager Brewing Company. This period was fundamental in developing my courtroom skills and legal expertise.

explicit_text: I served as Director of the Federal Judicial Center from 1990 to 1995. During my tenure, we focused on judicial education, research, and improving court facilities and personnel management. Leading the Center was a significant chapter in my career dedicated to enhancing the federal judiciary.
